main_sections.title: The Tinkling Bells
main_sections.prose_summary: Chinna is a bright, curious Grade 4 boy who lives in a village and loves his pet goat kid, Tara. One day on the way home from school, he spots a pair of beautiful tinkling bells at Chacha's shop priced at ten rupees. When he reaches into his pocket, he finds his money missing and returns home dejected. His mother Kamala comforts him gently, promising to give him money for the bells but asking him to be careful in future. They then go to the market together and buy fruits and vegetables. At the pineapple stall, the fruit seller accidentally gives Chinna ten rupees extra in change. Chinna whispers to his mother that he could use it for the bells and chocolates. Kamala firmly tells him this would be stealing and reminds him how sad he himself felt when he lost his money. Moved by her words, Chinna runs back and returns the extra money. The fruit seller is delighted and praises Chinna's honesty. Kamala rewards him by taking him to Chacha's shop just before it closes, where he buys the tinkling bells for ten rupees. On reaching home, Tara the kid comes running out hearing the bells, and Chinna happily ties them around her neck. The story's central themes are honesty, empathy, and the joy of doing the right thing.

question_bank.stem: Why was Chinna upset?
How did Kamala help Chinna feel better?
What did the fruit seller do by mistake?
How did Chinna manage to buy the bells from Chacha in the end?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: B. Write 'T' for true or 'F' for false.

question_bank.stem: Chinna wanted to buy the bells to attach them to his bicycle.
Chinna's mother, Kamala, comforted him when he came home feeling sad.
The fruit seller gave Chinna twenty rupees extra as change.
Chinna decided to keep the extra money and bought chocolates with it.
The fruit seller was unhappy when Chinna returned the extra money.

oracy.section_name: Let us Think + Let us Listen + Let us Speak

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
C. Think and discuss.
1. Why do you think Chinna decided to return the money instead of keeping it?
2. Think of a time when you found something that didn't belong to you. What did you do? How did it make you feel?

question_bank.stem: Why do you think Chinna decided to return the money instead of keeping it?
Think of a time when you found something that didn't belong to you. What did you do? How did it make you feel?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Your teacher will read out the story 'The Tinkling Bells' once again to you. Now read the following sentences and number them in the sequence of the story.
• Chinna discovered that his money was missing and felt sad.
• Chinna went to the market with his mother.
• Chinna hugged his mother and told her about the missing money.
• Chinna returned the extra money to the fruit seller.
• Chinna bought the tinkling bells after getting some money from his mother.
• Chinna wanted to buy bells for his pet at Chacha's shop.
tasks_verbatim.transcript_text: Teacher reads aloud the story 'The Tinkling Bells' in full. Learners listen and then sequence six given sentences in the correct story order: (1) Chinna wanted to buy bells for his pet at Chacha's shop; (2) Chinna discovered his money was missing and felt sad; (3) Chinna hugged his mother and told her about the missing money; (4) Chinna bought the tinkling bells after getting money from his mother; (5) Chinna went to the market with his mother; (6) Chinna returned the extra money to the fruit seller.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
What would you do in the following situations?
1. You borrowed your friend's favourite pen. After using it, you accidentally broke it. What would you do?
2. During a math quiz, your friends ask you to show them your answers because they didn't study. If you don't help, your friends might get upset with you. What would you do?
3. You see a classmate taking a notebook from the teacher's desk without permission. The classmate tells you not to tell anyone. What would you do?
4. You bring your favourite snack to school. But you find that your classmate has not brought anything to eat. You really want to enjoy the snack alone. What would you do?

question_bank.stem: You borrowed your friend's favourite pen. After using it, you accidentally broke it. What would you do?
During a math quiz, your friends ask you to show them your answers because they didn't study. If you don't help, your friends might get upset with you. What would you do?
You see a classmate taking a notebook from the teacher's desk without permission. The classmate tells you not to tell anyone. What would you do?
You bring your favourite snack to school. But you find that your classmate has not brought anything to eat. You really want to enjoy the snack alone. What would you do?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Find the given words.
[Word search grid with words: CALM, HONEST, LOVE, RESPECT, COURAGE, LOYAL, TRUTH, FAIR, KIND, PEACE]

visual_stimulus.brief_description: 12×12 letter grid for word search containing 10 value-related words: CALM, HONEST, LOVE, RESPECT, COURAGE, LOYAL, TRUTH, FAIR, KIND, PEACE.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. Read the following about degrees of comparison. Chinna, Charu and Jasmine are friends. They study in grade 4. Chinna is tall. Charu is taller than Chinna. Jasmine is the tallest person in the group.
[Grammar rule box: We use different forms of adjectives to compare the qualities of people or things. When we compare two people or objects, we add -er to the adjective and use 'than'. When comparison is made in a whole group, we add -est to the adjective.]
Fill in the blanks using the correct comparison markers (-er or -est).
1. I am tall....... than my brother.
2. January is the cold....... month of the year.
3. An ant is small....... than an elephant.
4. My sister is smart....... than me.
5. Jay was the fast....... person in the race.

question_bank.stem: I am tall....... than my brother.
January is the cold....... month of the year.
An ant is small....... than an elephant.
My sister is smart....... than me.
Jay was the fast....... person in the race.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Three illustrations showing Chinna alone (tall), Charu and Chinna together (taller), and the full group of three children (tallest) used to demonstrate degrees of comparison.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
C. Circle the word that means the opposite of the highlighted word in each sentence.
1. Chacha showed him some beautiful tinkling bells. a) Attractive  b) Ugly  c) Broken
2. The fruit seller was so happy that he gave Chinna ten rupees as a reward. a) Offer  b) Prize  c) Punishment
3. I'll give you money to buy the bells. a) Sell  b) Purchase  c) Order
4. "Can I buy chocolates with the money?" whispered Chinna. a) Murmured  b) Shouted  c) Talked

question_bank.stem: Chacha showed him some beautiful tinkling bells. Circle the opposite of 'beautiful'.
The fruit seller was so happy that he gave Chinna ten rupees as a reward. Circle the opposite of 'reward'.
I'll give you money to buy the bells. Circle the opposite of 'buy'.
"Can I buy chocolates with the money?" whispered Chinna. Circle the opposite of 'whispered'.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: B. Draw two national symbols (e.g., national flag, national bird) and write a few sentences about each.

beyond_text.section_name: Let us Do + Fun with Matchsticks + Let us Explore

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Create your own bell craft, similar to the bells Chinna wanted for his pet goat.
Materials Needed (Waste or Recyclable Items): Small metal bottle caps or small tin cans (cleaned and dried); String (about 12–15 inches for each bell); Old cardboard (for making a tag or decoration); Small pebbles, beans, or beads (for the tinkling sound); Aluminum foil (for decoration); Markers, crayons, or paint (optional); Glue or tape; Safety scissors; Hole punch or nail (with adult supervision).
Steps 1–11 provided: Fill cap/can with pebbles or beads; seal bottle cap or leave tin can open; thread string; tie knot; wrap in foil; decorate; make cardboard tag; write name or draw; thread tag onto string; adjust length; shake and enjoy.

Fun with Matchsticks — Activity: Fix the equation. Objective: Move one matchstick to make the equation correct. One puzzle has been solved for you (9 – 2 = 8 corrected to 8 – 2 = 6). Now, solve the following equation by moving one matchstick: [matchstick equation shown].

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Matchstick numeral equation puzzles: one solved example (9-2=8 corrected) and one unsolved puzzle for students to fix by moving one matchstick.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: We have read about Chinna's love for tinkling bells in the story. Collect or draw pictures of different types of bells found in India.
